summary: Spolsky mocks IT research firms like Forrester and Yankee Group for publishing inflated market projections that startups use to justify unrealistic business plans. He argues their growth extrapolations are essentially fabricated, based on minimal actual data projected forward with absurd growth rates. He illustrates how these projections enable a cycle where startups raise VC money based on capturing a tiny percentage of a fictional market, only to fail spectacularly. He closes with a cautionary note about venture capital, citing Philip Greenspun being pushed out of his own company by the VCs he brought in.
key_points: IT market research firms produce unreliable growth projections extrapolated from minimal data points
Startups use these inflated projections in business plans to justify raising venture capital with the 'just 1% of the market' argument
The cycle of VC-funded startups built on fantasy market projections leads predictably to failure
Venture capital comes with loss of control, as illustrated by Greenspun being ousted from ArsDigita
money_quotes: I hate those cheesy market research companies who come up with ridiculous numbers and extrapolations (suitable for business plans) that make no sense, which, frankly, they pulled out of their tokhes.

They always have these lame bar charts where they only have data for this year, then they extrapolate out four years assuming a typical 736.13% rate of growth, a number they divined by rolling dice and tossing bones about haphazardly.

So you show the stupid veecees the report from Forrester and they give you enough money to hire 250 people, and two years later you've got $254 in revenue from selling tomatoes you grew in the company backyard.
If your business plan includes projections from IT research firms, you better start planting tomatoes.
I was pushed out of ArsDigita by the venture capitalists and managers that I brought in.
key_insight: Market projections from IT research firms are fabricated nonsense that fuel a destructive cycle of VC-funded startups built on fantasy numbers.
